label: ch01.pdf
title: Chapter 1 Introduction to Data Communications and Networking

questions.text: The term **telecommunication** specifically refers to communication at a:
questions.explanation: As stated in the text, telecommunication means communication at a distance.

questions.text: Which effectiveness criterion of a data communications system refers to the variation in the packet arrival time?
questions.explanation: Jitter is defined as the variation in the packet arrival time, often causing uneven quality in audio/video.

questions.text: In a data communications system, what is the **protocol**?

choices.label: The device that sends the data
choices.text: The device that sends the data

choices.label: A set of rules that govern data communications
choices.text: A set of rules that govern data communications

questions.explanation: A protocol is defined as a set of rules that govern the exchange of data between devices.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the five components of a data communications system?
questions.explanation: The five components are Message, Sender, Receiver, Transmission medium, and Protocol. A switch is a network device but not a basic component listed in the model.

questions.text: In which mode of data flow is communication unidirectional, like a keyboard and monitor?
questions.explanation: Simplex mode is unidirectional, where only one device can transmit and the other can only receive.

questions.text: Walkie-talkies are an example of which type of data flow?
questions.explanation: In half-duplex, each station can both transmit and receive, but not at the same time.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a network where tasks are divided among multiple computers?
questions.explanation: Most networks use distributed computing, where a task is divided among multiple computers rather than one single machine.

questions.text: The amount of time required for a message to travel from one device to another is called:
questions.explanation: Transit time is the time for a message to travel between devices, whereas response time is the elapsed time between inquiry and response.

questions.text: Network reliability is measured by which of the following?
questions.explanation: Reliability is evaluated by how often a network fails, how long it takes to recover, and its robustness.

questions.text: In a **multipoint** connection, how is the link shared?

choices.label: A dedicated link between two devices
choices.text: A dedicated link between two devices

choices.label: More than two devices share a single link
choices.text: More than two devices share a single link

choices.label: Only one device uses the link at a time
choices.text: Only one device uses the link at a time

choices.label: The capacity is always reserved for one user
choices.text: The capacity is always reserved for one user

questions.explanation: Multipoint (or multidrop) connections allow more than two devices to share a single link.

questions.text: In a fully connected **mesh topology** with \( n \) devices, what is the number of physical links required for duplex communication?
questions.explanation: The formula for the number of duplex links in a mesh topology is \( n(n-1)/2 \).

questions.text: Which topology requires a central controller, usually called a **hub**?
questions.explanation: In a star topology, each device has a dedicated point-to-point link only to a central controller (hub).

questions.text: In a **bus topology**, nodes are connected to the main cable by:
questions.explanation: Nodes are connected to the bus cable via drop lines (the connection cable) and taps (the connector).

questions.text: In which topology is a signal passed along in one direction from device to device until it reaches its destination?
questions.explanation: In a ring topology, each device has a point-to-point connection with the devices on either side, and signals circulate in one direction.

questions.text: A **hybrid topology** consisting of a star backbone with three bus networks is described as:

choices.label: A main star with branches connecting to bus topologies
choices.text: A main star with branches connecting to bus topologies

questions.explanation: As shown in Figure 1.9, a hybrid topology can be a star backbone connecting several bus networks.

questions.text: Which type of network is typically privately owned and limited to a single office or building?
questions.explanation: LANs are privately owned and link devices in a single office, building, or campus.

questions.text: What is the typical speed of a modern LAN as mentioned in the text?
questions.explanation: The text states that LAN speeds are normally 100 or 1000 Mbps.

questions.text: A network covering a town or a city, such as a cable TV network, is classified as a:
questions.explanation: Metropolitan Area Networks (MAN) have a size between a LAN and a WAN, covering a city or town.

questions.text: A **Wide Area Network (WAN)** that connects a home computer to an ISP via a leased line is an example of a:
questions.explanation: A point-to-point WAN is a line leased from a provider connecting a home computer or small LAN to an ISP.

questions.text: The term **internet** (lowercase 'i') refers to:

choices.label: The global network we use today
choices.text: The global network we use today

choices.label: Two or more networks that can communicate with each other
choices.text: Two or more networks that can communicate with each other

questions.explanation: An internetwork (internet) is formed when two or more networks can communicate with each other.

questions.text: Which network was the first to use Interface Message Processors (IMPs)?
questions.explanation: ARPANET was the first network where computers were connected to specialized computers called IMPs.

questions.text: In the TCP/IP suite, which protocol is responsible for datagram routing?
questions.explanation: IP (Internetworking Protocol) handles datagram routing, while TCP handles segmentation and error detection.

questions.text: National ISPs are connected to each other by complex switching stations called:
questions.explanation: National ISPs are connected via Network Access Points (NAPs).

questions.text: What are private switching stations that national ISPs use to connect, typically operating at high data rates up to 600 Mbps?
questions.explanation: National ISPs can connect via private switching stations called peering points.

questions.text: Which level of ISP provides direct service to end users?
questions.explanation: Local ISPs provide direct service to the end users and can be connected to regional or national ISPs.

questions.text: In the context of protocols, **Syntax** refers to:

choices.label: The structure or format of the data
choices.text: The structure or format of the data

choices.label: The speed at which data is sent
choices.text: The speed at which data is sent

choices.label: The route taken by the message
choices.text: The route taken by the message

questions.explanation: Syntax refers to the structure or format of the data, such as specifying that the first 8 bits are the source address.

questions.text: Which element of a protocol explains the meaning of each section of bits and what action is to be taken?
questions.explanation: Semantics refers to the meaning of each section of bits and how they should be interpreted.

questions.text: The **Timing** element of a protocol is concerned with:

choices.label: When data should be sent and how fast it can be sent
choices.text: When data should be sent and how fast it can be sent

questions.explanation: Timing defines when data should be sent and how fast it can be transmitted to match speeds between sender and receiver.

questions.text: Standards that have not been approved by an organized body but are widely used are called:
questions.explanation: De facto standards are those adopted through widespread use without official legislative approval.

questions.text: Which organization is an officially recognized body that legislates **De jure** standards?
questions.explanation: ISO, ITU-T, and ANSI are all examples of officially recognized standards creation committees.

questions.text: What is the name of the government agency that regulates radio and communications technology in the US?
questions.explanation: The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) is the regulatory agency in the US.

questions.text: A specification that begins as a working document with no official status and a 6-month lifetime is called an:
questions.explanation: An Internet Draft is a work in progress with no official status and a 6-month lifetime.

questions.text: What does **RFC** stand for in the context of Internet standards?
questions.explanation: RFC stands for Request for Comment, which is a document published for review by interested parties.

questions.text: In a mesh topology with 10 devices, how many duplex links are needed?
The primary disadvantage of a **mesh topology** is:

choices.label: If one link fails, the whole system fails
choices.text: If one link fails, the whole system fails

choices.label: The amount of cabling and number of I/O ports required
choices.text: The amount of cabling and number of I/O ports required

choices.label: Signals only travel in one direction
choices.text: Signals only travel in one direction

questions.explanation: Because every device must be connected to every other device, the massive amount of wiring and ports makes it expensive and difficult to install.

questions.text: Which topology is commonly used in LANs today where devices connect to a central hub?
questions.explanation: The star topology is the most common topology used in modern Local Area Networks (LANs).

questions.text: The delivery of data as they are produced, in the same order and without significant delay, is a requirement for:
questions.explanation: Real-time transmission requires delivering data as it is produced, specifically for video and audio.

questions.text: What is the purpose of an **Interface Message Processor (IMP)** in the original ARPANET?

choices.label: To act as the main host computer
choices.text: To act as the main host computer

choices.label: To connect the host to the network and communicate with other IMPs
choices.text: To connect the host to the network and communicate with other IMPs

choices.label: To provide a user interface for scientists
choices.text: To provide a user interface for scientists

questions.explanation: IMPs were specialized computers that connected host computers to the network and communicated with other IMPs to route packets.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a **Point-to-point** connection?

choices.label: A dedicated link between two devices
choices.text: A dedicated link between two devices

choices.label: A single cable shared by many devices
choices.text: A single cable shared by many devices

choices.label: A wireless broadcast to all devices
choices.text: A wireless broadcast to all devices

choices.label: A connection using a central hub
choices.text: A connection using a central hub

questions.explanation: A point-to-point connection provides a dedicated link between two devices.

questions.text: Protecting data from unauthorized access and damage is known as network:
questions.explanation: Network security involves protecting data from unauthorized access, damage, and implementing recovery policies.
